title: World: Epidemic and emerging disease alerts in the Pacific as of 26 May 2026
summary: Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 19 May 2026: Dengue Cook Islands: Dengue transmission in the Cook Islands has been interrupted, with no new cases reported since 28 April 2026. Since 1st January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking.
bullets: Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 19 May 2026: Dengue Cook Islands: Dengue transmission in the Cook Islands has been interrupted, with no new cases reported since 28 April 2026.

Since 1st January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking.

The Ministry of Health officially declared the country dengue-free on 25 May 2026 after more than two incubation periods (26 days) without any reported cases, meeting the criteria for declaring the end of the dengue outbreak.
This follows the scale-down of Operation Namu26 on 24 April 2026, with routine surveillance and vector control measures continuing to sustain the dengue-free status.
– Source: Cook Islands Dengue Free - Te Marae Ora Cook Islands Ministry of Health accessed on 26 May 2026.

full_text:
Highlights/updates since the last map was sent on PacNet on 19 May 2026:

Dengue

Cook Islands:
 Dengue transmission in the Cook Islands has been interrupted, with no new cases reported since 28 April 2026. The Ministry of Health officially declared the country dengue-free on 25 May 2026 after more than two incubation periods (26 days) without any reported cases, meeting the criteria for declaring the end of the dengue outbreak. This follows the scale-down of Operation Namu26 on 24 April 2026, with routine surveillance and vector control measures continuing to sustain the dengue-free status. 
The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 is removed. – 
Source:
 
Cook Islands Dengue Free - Te Marae Ora Cook Islands Ministry of Health
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

Kiribati:
 Dengue transmission continues in Kiribati, with the outbreak ongoing since April 2025. As of 26 May 2026, a cumulative total of 535 laboratory-confirmed cases and 1,583 suspected cases have been reported nationwide, including 20 new confirmed cases and 19 new suspected cases since 13 April 2026. Transmission remains concentrated in South Tarawa, with both DENV-1 and DENV-2 circulating, and most cases reported as mild, although one death and 68 hospitalisations have been recorded to date. 
The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect. –
 
Source: Dengue Fever Situational Report 33 shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 26 May 2026.

New Caledonia:
 As of 23 May 2026, dengue transmission remains ongoing in New Caledonia, with 1,837 cases reported in 2026. The outbreak is driven by locally acquired infections, with DENV-1 as the predominant serotype. Environmental conditions favourable to mosquito breeding, together with population movement, continue to support transmission, and health authorities are reinforcing early care-seeking and vector control measures. 
The red alert for DENV-1 remains in effect.
 – 
Source:
 
La dengue | Direction des Affaires Sanitaires et Sociales de Nouvelle-Calédonie
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

Samoa:
 As of EpiWeek 20 (11–17 May 2026), dengue transmission continues in Samoa, with 141 new cases reported, representing a 7% increase compared with the previous week, and eight new hospital admissions with no ICU cases or deaths. DENV-1 (54%) and DENV-2 (46%) continue to co-circulate, with transmission widespread across Upolu (64%) and Savai‘i (36%), and children under 15 years accounting for the majority of cases (74%). Since 1st January 2025, a cumulative total of 19,029 clinically diagnosed cases and 5,446 laboratory-confirmed cases have been reported, with nine dengue-related deaths to date, while public health measures continue to focus on community awareness, vector control and early healthcare seeking. 
The blue alert for DENV-1 and DENV-2 remains in effect. 
– Source:
 
Dengue-sitrep-issue-no-57
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

Tonga:
 As of 25 May 2026, dengue transmission continues in Tonga, with a total of 66 laboratory-confirmed cases reported, including two new cases since the 22 May 2026. Two cases are currently hospitalised, with no dengue-related deaths reported to date. The epidemiological profile remains unchanged, with the highest burden among children aged 11–14 years, predominantly male, and most cases reported from Tongatapu. DENV‑2 remains the identified circulating serotype, and response activities, including community awareness campaigns and vector control measures, are ongoing to limit further transmission. 
The red alert for DENV-2 remains in effect.
 
– Source:
 
Dengue Fever Outbreak Situation Report #23 - Ministry of Health Tonga - Official Facebook page
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

Pertussis/Whooping cough

New Zealand:
 Pertussis activity in New Zealand continues as part of the ongoing national epidemic, with transmission persisting nationwide. As of the week ending 15 May 2026, 48 cases were reported, an increase compared with 44 cases in the previous week. This brings the cumulative total for 2026 to 734 cases. 
The blue alert for pertussis remains in effect.
 
– Source:
 
Pertussis dashboard
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

Other Information:

Dengue

Federated States of Micronesia, Kosrae State:
 Dengue activity in Kosrae State, Federated States of Micronesia, remains under monitoring, with three cases reported over the past two weeks, including two newly confirmed and unlinked cases detected through laboratory surveillance. The affected individuals have been managed clinically, with cases reported as isolated, treated and discharged, and no evidence of widespread transmission identified to date. Given the presence of the mosquito vector on the island, public health authorities are maintaining surveillance and implementing vector control and community clean-up measures to reduce the risk of further transmission. 
– Source: FSM weekly surveillance report (May 4-14, 2026 – EpiWeek 19 & 20) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 22 May 2026.

New Zealand:
 In New Zealand, during EpiWeek 20 (16 – 22 May 2026), two confirmed imported dengue cases were reported. The confirmed cases had each been to the Cook Islands and Samoa during the incubation periods. Given the known serological cross‑reactivity between dengue and other flaviviruses, some reported dengue cases may ultimately be reclassified as other flavivirus infections as epidemiological and laboratory investigations continue. 
– Source: NZ Arbovirus Notifications by Country (20: 16/05/2026–22/05/2026) shared with PPHSN Coordinating Body Focal point on 25 May
 2026.

Vanuatu:
 From EpiWeek 01 to EpiWeek 19, 2026, a total of eight dengue cases have been reported in Vanuatu, all on Efate Island, with no hospitalisations or deaths recorded. Cases are primarily concentrated in the Pango Area Council (88%), with the remaining cases reported in Port Vila, and epidemiological investigations have identified links between several cases, including recent travel to Fiji within the incubation period. Although case numbers remain below the alert threshold and no outbreak has been declared, a slight increase in cases has been observed and health authorities are continuing close surveillance to monitor potential changes in transmission. 
– Source: Vanuatu Dengue Situation Update 01 (18 May 2026) on PacNet on 26 May
 2026.

Diphtheria

Australia:
 Diphtheria activity in Australia has increased markedly since late 2025, with 221 confirmed cases reported in 2026 as of 18 May, representing a substantial rise compared with previous years. The outbreak is concentrated in the Northern Territory (60.2%) and Western Australia (35.7%), with most cases locally acquired and occurring in remote and very remote areas, particularly among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations. Both cutaneous (67.9%) and respiratory (31.7%) presentations are reported, with no confirmed diphtheria-related deaths reported to date in 2026, although one death remains under investigation, and public health response measures including enhanced surveillance, vaccination, contact tracing and infection control are ongoing. 
– Sources:
 
Diphtheria outbreak update, 
Diphtheria declared a Communicable Disease Incident of National Significance
 and 
Diphtheria in Australia – Epidemiological update – 18 May 2026
 
accessed on 26 May
 2026.

Ebola virus

Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda:
 As of 21–22 May 2026, the outbreak of Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda remains ongoing and has been designated a Public Health Emergency of International Concern by WHO on May 16, 2026, with 85 confirmed cases and 10 confirmed deaths reported across both countries, including two imported confirmed cases and one death in Uganda. In DRC, 83 confirmed cases and 746 suspected cases, including 176 suspected deaths, have been reported across Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu provinces, with transmission concentrated in Mongbwalu, Rwampara and Bunia. High population mobility with humanitarian challenges, weak contact follow-up and inadequate isolation and referral systems continue to complicate response efforts. With no licensed vaccines or specific therapeutics currently available for Bundibugyo virus disease, response measures continue to focus on surveillance, contact tracing, infection prevention and control, case management, safe and dignified burials, and cross-border coordination, while WHO temporary recommendations do not currently advise travel or border closures. 
– Sources:
 
Epidemic of Ebola Disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda determined a public health emergency of international concern,
 
Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus, Democratic Republic of the Congo & Uganda
 
and
 
Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus – Democratic Republic of the Congo
 
accessed on 26 May
 2026.

Murray Valley Encephalitis

Australia, Northern Territory:
 Recent detections of Murray Valley encephalitis (MVE) virus have been reported in the Northern Territory, including two confirmed cases in Alice Springs, both of whom died, and virus detection in mosquitoes in the Greater Darwin region. Increased rainfall during the wet season has contributed to higher mosquito populations, elevating the risk of transmission of this rare but potentially severe mosquito-borne infection affecting the central nervous system. Enhanced mosquito surveillance and control activities are ongoing, and clinicians are advised to consider MVE in patients presenting with compatible symptoms, particularly during the seasonal risk period from February to July. – 
Source:
 
Murray Valley encephalitis | NT Health
 
accessed on 26 May 2026.

related.excerpt: THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 THE GENEV A CONVENTIONS OF 12 AUGUST 1949 ICRC 0173/002 05.2010 10,000 Mission The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) is an impartial, neutral and independent organization whose exclusively humanitarian mission is to protect the lives and dignity of victims of armed conflict and other situations of violence and to provide them with assistance. The ICRC also endeavours to prevent suffering by promoting and strengthening humanitarian law and universal humanitarian principles. Established in 1863, the ICRC is at the origin of the Geneva Conventions and the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement. It directs and coordinates the international activities conducted by the Movement in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. related.title: United Nations Charter
related.summary: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.
bullets: CHAPTER II MEMBERSHIP Article 3 The original Members of the United Nations shall be the states which, having participated in the United Nations Conference on International Organization at San Francisco, or having previ- ously signed the Declaration by United Nations of January 1,1942, sign the present Charter and ratify it in accordance with Article 110.

Article 5 / A Member of the United Nations against which preventive or enforcement action has been taken by the Security Council may be suspended from the exercise of the rights and privileges of mem- bership by the General Assembly upon the recom- mendation of the Security Council.

Article 6 A Member of the United Nations which has persistently violated the Principles contained in the present Charter may be expelled from the Organization by the General Assembly upon the recommendation of the Security Council.

Accordingly, our respective Governments, through representatives assembled in the city of San Francisco, who have exhibited their full powers found to be in good and due form, have agreed to the present Charter of the United Nations and do hereby establish an international organization to be known as the United Nations.
CHAPTER I PURPOSES AND PRINCIPLES Article 1 The^Purposes of the United Nations are: 1.
related.excerpt: CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS AND STATUTE OF THE INTERNATIONAL COURT OF JUSTICE SAN FRANCISCO • 1945 CHARTER OF THE UNITED NATIONS WE THE PEOPLES OF THE UNITED NATIONS DETERMINED to save succeeding generations from the scourge of war, which twice in our life- time has brought untold sorrow to mankind, and to reaffirm faith in fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law can be maintained, and to promote social progress and better standards of life in larger freedom, AND FOR THESE ENDS to practice tolerance and live together in peace with one another as good neighbors, and to unite our strength to maintain international peace and security, and to ensure, by the acceptance of principles and the institution of methods, that armed force shall not be used, save in the common interest, and to employ international machinery for the promotion of the economic and social advancement of all peoples, HAVE RESOLVED TO COMBINE OUR EFFORTS TO ACCOMPLISH THESE AIMS

related.title: UN Peacekeeping Capstone Doctrine
related.summary: 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking. During this time, the conduct of United Nations peacekeeping operations has been guided by a largely unwritten body of principles and informed by the experiences of the many thousands of men and women who have served in the more than 60 operations launched since 1948. United Nations Peacekeeping Operations Principles and Guidelines 2008 Over the past six decades, United Nations peacekeeping has evolved into a complex, global undertaking.

Since 2010, Kakolele has been involved in activities apparently on behalf of the DRC government’s Programme de Stabilisation et Reconstruction des Zones Sortant des Conflits Armés (STAREC), including participation in a STAREC mission to Goma and Beni in March 2011. DRC authorities arrested him in December 2013 in Beni, North Kivu Province, for allegedly blocking the DDR process. He left the DRC and lived in Kenya for some time, before being called back by the DRC Government to assist them with the situation in the Territory of Beni. related.title: Bangladesh seeks IMF aid: How badly has Iran war hit its economy?
related.summary: However, in March, the government of Bangladesh said it was seeking $2bn in loans from various donors as it grappled with an energy crisis caused by the war on Iran. Furthermore, the Strait of Hormuz – through which before the war one-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supplies were shipped, largely to Asian countries – remains under the control of Iran while the US has a naval blockade of Iranian ports in place.
bullets: However, in March, the government of Bangladesh said it was seeking $2bn in loans from various donors as it grappled with an energy crisis caused by the war on Iran.

Furthermore, the Strait of Hormuz – through which before the war one-fifth of the world’s oil and natural gas supplies were shipped, largely to Asian countries – remains under the control of Iran while the US has a naval blockade of Iranian ports in place.
Energy crisis The Iran war, which began on February 28 when the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran, has caused a worldwide energy crisis and sent fuel prices soaring.
The International Monetary Fund (IMF) reports that Bangladesh has requested a new assistance programme as it struggles with the economic fallout of the United States-Israel war on Iran.
All this has caused major disruptions to energy supplies worldwide and caused the price of oil to rise to about $100 a barrel, compared with its pre-war price of about $66.
related.excerpt: The International Monetary Fund (IMF) reports that Bangladesh has requested a new assistance programme as it struggles with the economic fallout of the United States-Israel war on Iran. We take a look at what assistance Bangladesh has sought, the South Asian country’s history with the IMF and how the war has hit its economy. What has Bangladesh asked for? The IMF’s mission chief for Bangladesh, Ivo Krznar, announced on Tuesday that Bangladesh had requested a new IMF-supported programme. “IMF staff are in discussions with the authorities on their reform agenda and policy priorities,” Krznar said in a statement. “The IMF remains a committed partner to Bangladesh in its efforts to secure lasting macroeconomic and financial stability, strengthen resilience, and support strong, inclusive growth.” Neither side disclosed the size or precise terms of the requested financial aid package. However, in March, the government of Bangladesh said it was seeking $2bn in loans from various donors as it grappled with an energy crisis caused by the war on Iran. How badly has Bangladesh been hit by the Iran war? Energy crisis The Iran war, which began on February 28 when the US and Israel launched stri
